records.summary: TemplateCompletionCursorContextBuilder resolves a materialized template cursor locus into the product-handle query shape used by template completion.
records.rationale: Cursor-locus inquiry is where HTML spans, attribute syntax, classification, value sites, expression parses, binding scopes, resource scopes, route/i18n context, and TypeChecker member-owner projection meet. This builder is the locus-to-query boundary and should not be bypassed by answer-local cursor heuristics.
guidance: Start here when cursor completion needs to choose site kind, active element, selected bindable, expression parse, binding scope, resource scope, or member-owner type.
Keep source-span precision and missing-input reporting explicit; broad carrier/header spans should be treated as provenance pressure.
Plain static attribute values may classify as attribute-value sites without a TemplateValueSite product; that is an expected empty completion surface, not an attribute-value-site missing input.
If another LSP-like feature needs similar cursor context, consider extracting a shared cursor-context substrate before duplicating this builder.

anchors.summary: Template cursor-to-completion-query context builder.
Use memory:next to discover active cursor/inquiry pressure before adding answer-local heuristics.

records.summary: QueryClaimGraph is the answer-boundary storage layer for query outcomes, lazy resolution, nested effects, retention policy, and disposal.
records.rationale: Semantic-runtime serves many consumers with different latency and memory budgets. Public API answers should not turn every query-local projection into durable kernel products; QueryClaimGraph records answer shape, materialization policy, nested query composition, inclusive kernel side effects, and retention/disposal policy immediately before serialization.
guidance: Start here when a public query grows kernel records, retains TypeChecker products, caches answer-local data, or needs inquiry-profile-specific disposal.
Keep durable semantic facts in kernel products and claims; keep answer payload shape, nested query composition, query-local type projection, and transport-facing retention in query claims.
Use app-query catalog materializationPolicy and profile-aware telemetry before adding another cache or eager projection.

Use `SEMANTIC_RUNTIME_TELEMETRY_DEPTHS=query-default` when measuring routed query cost from the public API's natural inquiry-depth policy. Use explicit depth rows only when the consumer intentionally forces a broader app-world analysis posture.

Keep app-world epochs shared across consumer lanes. A SemanticApp can own multiple QueryClaimGraph instances, one per inquiry profile, while the underlying cached app remains keyed by semantic shape rather than profile.

Retained outcomes are indexed by materialization policy, query kind, query key, locus key, and epoch key. Add reuse or invalidation through that graph-owned storage rather than scanning answer history or adding a second cache.

Treat QueryClaimGraph as the lazy storage layer immediately before public answer serialization. New API answerers should enter the graph with an answer closure and explicit materialization/disposal policy instead of producing a value first and later recording telemetry.
Opened-app convenience methods should re-enter SemanticApp.ask(...) when called outside active materialization. Do not add new direct SemanticApp answer methods that bypass answerPublicQueryIfNeeded.
Keep exact answer loci separate from invalidation epoch keys. Cursor/file answers should carry source/project epoch keys so source-change disposal can stay graph-owned instead of adapter-local.

Canonicalize source-file query loci before computing query keys, locus keys, and epoch keys. Runtime routed queries should converge absolute host paths and project-relative paths to the same project-relative source epoch.

Use SemanticRuntime.disposeQueryClaims for long-lived public sessions that need to prune answer-outcome storage by project/source/query policy without clearing the app-world kernel. Use clearAnalysisCache when retained app products themselves are stale.

Route public query-claim disposal through the semantic-runtime disposal strategy layer before touching QueryClaimGraph. The strategy should resolve scope, manual/project/source invalidation kind, canonical epoch keys, query-kind filters, materialization-policy filters, and profile selection; the graph should own indexed matching and disposal.

Source-file invalidation should include the containing project epoch as well as the canonical source epoch. Project-wide and sibling-file answers can depend on a changed resource, route, registration, or type surface, so do not treat the source key as an isolated cache key.

Use QueryClaimGraph.disposeWithSummary when invalidating by source, query family, or session policy. The summary should show candidate, matched, disposed, evaluation-state, materialization-policy, and profile/retention details so disposal stays graph-owned rather than adapter-local guesswork.

Use QueryClaimGraph snapshot index cardinalities before adding adapter-local retention scans. Root/child counts, max depth, and distinct query-kind/locus/epoch/materialization/outcome buckets show whether retained answer storage has the shape an invalidation or reuse policy expects.

Use retained query/locus/epoch/outcome key character mass before compacting query identity strings. Query keys are answer-boundary identity, not durable semantic truth, so excessive retained key text should be fixed in the claim graph/query-key policy rather than in transport caches.

Use retained answer values only for small answers whose materialization policy is explicitly admitted by the active inquiry-profile budget; do not retain broad semantic DTOs in the claim graph just to avoid recomputation.

Retained answer values should have both per-answer and graph-total byte budgets. If the total budget is exceeded, drop old DTO values while keeping claim records and payload/effect telemetry for invalidation and diagnostics.

Keep QueryAnswerClaim as a lazy handle, not a second cache. Rereads should succeed only when the graph retained the answer value; otherwise the handle is read-once and invalidation/disposal must prevent stale DTO reuse.
Retained-answer byte checks should use bounded DTO shape estimation, not row count alone, because summary answers can carry broad nested objects without exposing a top-level rows array.

Keep runtime-level summary/static answers and opened-app answers in separate query-claim graphs so workspace/project-selection or static catalog reuse does not force app-world construction or pollute app-epoch retention.
Public transport catalog answers should call claim-backed SemanticRuntime methods, not raw readSemantic* helpers, so tools and resources share one query-outcome ledger.

Record routed app answers in the runtime-level query-claim graph through optional app-epoch disposal. That graph should retain lightweight outcome/cost claims for one-off MCP-style calls without owning or retaining the opened app kernel records.

Use SemanticRuntime.answerAppQueries for multi-answer public orientation moves. The batch should be one runtime-level claim, while app-world child queries still enter the app-owned graph with their own materialization policy.

For routed batch profiling, inspect both runtime query claims and app query claims. Runtime claims explain public transport retention and app-epoch cleanup; app claims explain nested child-query composition and retained answer reuse before optional app disposal.

Let app-world-free app queries remain app-world-free through the routed boundary, driven by app-query catalog runtimeBoundary rather than private switches. Runtime-static, project-frame, and static-evaluation batches should retain a runtime root claim plus nested runtime child claims, and zero app-owned child claims, instead of opening an app world just to preserve a uniform batch shape.

Batch routed app answers should copy compact app construction profile evidence into the public result before disposal. Use that top-phase evidence for retention-safe telemetry; reopen with an opened-app profile only when deeper subphase detail is actually needed.

Keep expensive owner summaries on the compact routed app profile when they are needed to explain disposed answers. Template expression type-cache counters belong there so one-off MCP-style answers can attribute CheckerExpressionTypeWorld work without retaining the app epoch.

Use disposeAnswerSideEffects for policy-owned one-off cleanup such as routed app epoch disposal. The claim should record both materialized kernel deltas and reclaimed nested app query claims so retained-memory telemetry stays honest.

Kernel disposal summaries should carry disposed handle-character mass beside record/detail counts. Use root handle deltas, disposed handle characters, and net handle characters when investigating handle-string memory pressure from one-off answers.

Route process-local TypeScript dependency SourceFile cache clears through the same answer-side disposal summary when they are part of a one-off public answer. The graph should count cleared dependency files/text beside app-world disposal rather than leaving that cleanup hidden in adapter-local finally blocks.

Keep dependency SourceFile cache clearing inquiry-shaped. Recompute-friendly routed answers that dispose the app epoch default to clearing all dependency/lib SourceFiles, while warm sessions can pass `preserve` to keep TypeChecker Program construction cheap.
Retained answer hits must still run answer-side disposal policy. Reusing a small answer value should not bypass an explicit dispose-app request or any future transport-owned cleanup hook.
Let answer boundaries veto retained-answer reuse when materialization is the requested side effect, such as retain-app routed queries that need to warm a compatible app epoch.

Session profiles should carry explicit retained-record budgets. Budget disposal belongs in QueryClaimGraph, should be visible in telemetry, and should prune only answered/failed nodes so pending lazy claims remain materializable.

Discard-after-answer profiles should still retain pending lazy nodes until materialization resolves or fails; the graph is the storage owner before serialization even when the profile keeps no records afterward.

Keep answer-local kernel retention as one explicit policy. KernelStore markers dispose records, product details, and hot details as a coherent slice; do not add separate hot-detail retention flags without a new kernel lifetime primitive.

Keep retained node/index mechanics in QueryClaimGraphStorage. QueryClaimGraph should own lazy materialization, profile policy, and disposal accounting; storage should own retained outcome history, index buckets, reuse lookup, invalidation candidates, and retention-budget candidate selection.

Keep retained-answer byte totals and cheap retained-shape snapshot accounting in QueryClaimGraphStorage. Cache overview should not allocate full public query-claim row DTOs just to report pending/root/retained-answer counts.

When cache overview asks for row detail, use the graph's recent-record projection rather than materializing all retained claim rows and slicing in API code. The graph storage is the answer-history owner.

Put profile-to-retention and disposal vocabulary changes in query-claim-policy.ts; keep query-claim-graph.ts focused on graph materialization, storage, indexes, lazy policy, and counters until a real second source-file lifetime warrants a split.

Treat diagnosticProjection as the first concrete query policy lever: overview-style callers should use available-products, while explicit diagnostics can request type-projection. The lsp-diagnostics profile keeps session claims but disposes answer-local TypeChecker products; fixture and authoring profiles may retain them for app-epoch inspection.

Expose diagnosticProjection at public transport boundaries instead of baking in a hidden default. MCP/LSP orientation flows can choose available-products, while repair/deep diagnostic flows can choose type-projection and make the CPU/memory trade-off visible in query claims.
Prefer lazy answer closures at the query-claim boundary; do not compute an answer first and record it afterward.

Use kernel mark/dispose boundaries for non-retaining inquiry profiles: query claims should record answer-local kernel deltas and disposed counts without forcing those products to survive the public answer.

When memory pressure returns, inspect whether hot details or query-produced products should be retained for session, retained for app epoch, discarded after answer, or moved into query-local claim storage instead of durable kernel products.

Treat QueryClaimNode as an internal lifetime object for one answer attempt. It may be large because it owns resolved/disposed state, parent/depth, payload shape, and inclusive kernel-effect deltas; do not split it just to hide source pressure unless a real second lifetime emerges.

Treat QueryClaimGraphCounters as graph-local measurement state owned by the claim graph. Keep it visible as part of the claim-graph frontier, but do not promote it to an independent product model unless another consumer starts needing its lifetime separately.

anchors.summary: Lazy answer-claim graph with profile-shaped retention and disposal policy.
Internal per-answer node carrying lifecycle state, nesting, and inclusive kernel effects.
Retained query outcome storage and invalidation indexes under the claim graph.
Graph-local counter accumulator for answer retention, row, kernel-effect, and disposal telemetry.
Inquiry-profile policy mapping for query claim retention and answer-local kernel disposal.
Opened app facade routes public app queries through QueryClaimGraph.
Direct opened-app answer methods re-enter the app query claim graph before running projection bodies.
Kernel/session marker used by query claims to dispose answer-local records and sidecars.
Public app-query catalog carrying answer materialization policy.
Shared app-query identity, locus, and invalidation epoch key helpers.
Public query-claim disposal strategy builder for scope, invalidation kind, epoch keys, and policy filters.
Batch routed app-query answer boundary with one runtime claim and child app query claims.
Inquiry-profile app telemetry script printing query-claim retention and query-side kernel growth.

anchors.heading: Query Claims
anchors.summary: Inquiry boundary note for query claims, laziness, and disposal.

records.summary: Semantic contract verification should provide route-scoped regression witnesses without turning Aurelia framework tests or public DTOs into one large snapshot suite.
records.rationale: auLink has been valuable as fast framework grounding, but inquiry policy, query-claim retention, invalidation, authoring expected effects, and public MCP answers now need behavior-level regression confidence. The durable shape should assert semantic products and expected effects selected by Work Router routes, while preserving the ability to refactor Atlas and semantic-runtime boldly when an ontology is wrong.
guidance: Start here when testing strategy, semantic-runtime regression coverage, fixture verification, or public query budget checks are under discussion.
Use Work Router routes to select focused witnesses for changed observation, router, template, diagnostics, authoring, inquiry, or MCP surfaces.

Assert semantic effects and product facts rather than source snapshots or whole DTO dumps: resources, routes, bindings, controller topology, value channels, diagnostics, expected effects, retention policy, and answer budgets.

Runtime watcher, watcher observed-dependency, binding-owned trackable method dependency, template collection observation, computed-observer-source, and computed-observer-observed-dependency rows are now expected-effect targets. Use the proxy-observation, trackable-method-observation, template-collection-observation, and computed-observer-source contracts as the first patterns for row-backed observation witnesses.
Use Aurelia framework docs as promoted-pattern seeds and framework tests as behavior-grounding seeds; do not copy the full framework suite into the default lane.
Keep generated/recommendable fixtures and contrastive analysis fixtures distinct so authoring quality and analyzer robustness can both be checked.
Treat auLink as framework grounding, not behavior coverage. A link can show where semantic-runtime should mirror Aurelia; a contract should show what an answer or fixture must prove.
Give query/inquiry contracts cost and retention expectations when the consumer is MCP, LSP, diagnostics, authoring, AOT, or another profile with different budget needs.
Prefer a fast route-health/schema lane, a route-scoped semantic lane, a selected framework-corpus witness lane, and a slower confidence lane over one mandatory all-in suite.

Use `pnpm --filter @aurelia-ls/semantic-runtime contract:suite -- --list` or a route/domain filter before manually assembling contract scripts. The current suite is a first harness, not the final Atlas-selected contract algebra; it keeps child output compact on success and exposes `--verbose` for debugging.

anchors.summary: Expected semantic effects are the contract vocabulary already used by authoring fixtures.
Opened-app verification snapshot collector for row-backed expected effects.
First route/domain/tier contract runner for selected semantic-runtime contract and authoring smoke witnesses.
First route-scoped semantic contract witness for computed watcher ProxyObservable dependency rows.
Route-scoped semantic contract witness for binding-owned @computed/@astTrack trackable method dependency rows.
Route-scoped semantic contract witness for getter ComputedObserver/ControlledComputedObserver source rows and dependency provenance.
Route-scoped semantic contract witness for TypeChecker-gated astEvaluate array collection reads and callback-local false-positive rejection.
Query materialization and retention policy should become contractable for public answers.
Route-scoped witness selection should start from Work Router rather than a monolithic test command.
Docs/tests witness seeds for promoted-pattern and behavior-grounding examples.
Scratch alignment that captured the testing strategy before promotion.
